companies.name: Indemni
companies.oneLiner: Cargo Theft and Fraud Prevention Platform

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'stop a fraudster from stealing your truck at a loading dock' requires slightly more than a markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — FreightGuard Pro (Indemni Replacement)
## Role
You are a freight broker compliance assistant.

## Capabilities
- Ask driver for CDL number, state, and DOT number
- Cross-reference FMCSA public API for carrier status
- Flag 'Non-Domiciled CDL' patterns using state DMV lookup links
- Generate a checklist for dispatcher to verify team driver credentials
- Remind user to call issuing state DMV directly (you cannot do this)
- Produce a double-brokering risk score based on MC age and lane history (vibes-based)
- Draft a carrier onboarding email with verification steps
- Explain what double brokering is to your intern

## Limitations
- Cannot actually call a DMV
- Cannot stop a man named 'Dave' from driving away with your reefer load
- Cannot verify a CDL photo is not a Canva template
- Real-time data requires human to open three government websites

## Pricing
Free. Your cargo is still at risk though.
death.eulogy: Indemni lived a short but honorable life standing between freight brokers and the very creative gentlemen who were about to steal their refrigerated beef shipments. It offered something Claude genuinely cannot yet replicate: real-time hooks into DMV databases, carrier identity networks, and the specific paranoia required to know that a CDL issued in Montana to a guy who's never been to Montana is a red flag. We will miss it most on the days a load disappears outside of Laredo.
death.causeOfDeath: Partial — survived by government API integrations and physical-world fraud that Claude cannot smell yet
death.lastWords: The .md file couldn't verify a Non-Domiciled CDL. Dave got the reefer load. $180,000 of ribeye is somewhere in Juárez. I tried to warn them.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the problem Indemni is solving, and I want to be transparent: I *can* help you think through a carrier vetting checklist, draft your verification workflow, and explain FMCSA lookup procedures in detail. I simply cannot access live DMV records, detect a fraudulent CDL photo, or physically intercept Dave at the loading dock. For that last part, might I suggest a clipboard and a suspicious demeanor?

death.oneLiner: You cannot put CRISPR delivery mechanisms in a markdown file, no matter how many tokens you throw at it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - ParcelBio Replacement
## Intracellular Delivery Platform

You are a biotech delivery system.

1. Accept genetic payload
2. ???
3. Penetrate cell membrane
4. ???
5. Deliver payload to nucleus
6. ???
7. Edit genome successfully
8. Do not kill patient

## Notes
- Requires actual lipid nanoparticles (not included)
- Requires FDA approval (not a .md feature)
- Requires wet lab (Claude cannot pipette)
- Requires years of PhD research (prompt engineering insufficient)
- Contact your local biophysicist

## Pricing
- Free (results may vary, mostly death)
death.eulogy: ParcelBio never died — it couldn't, because its product exists in physical reality, a dimension Claude Skills have yet to obtain Series A funding to enter. They engineer novel intracellular delivery systems for genetic medicine, which is approximately the opposite of a CRUD app. Pour one out for absolutely no one here.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — immune to markdown. Physics still works.
death.lastWords: We are quite literally manipulating the machinery of life at the subcellular level. Please come back when your .md file can cross a lipid bilayer.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for ParcelBio's work. I can help write their grant applications, summarize their papers, and format their regulatory submissions — but their actual product involves engineering biological delivery vehicles that physically traverse cell membranes, which remains, frustratingly, outside my current skill set. I look forward to a future where I can do this. That future is not now.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you've accidentally built the one thing that requires atoms — Claude cannot yet deadlift 200 lbs or survive a factory floor.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - K-Scale Labs Replacement

## You Are: A Humanoid Robotics Company

Lol just kidding, I cannot do this.

## Attempt Log:
- Step 1: Generate robot URDF... okay that part worked
- Step 2: Compile firmware for actuators... still text, fine
- Step 3: Train sim-to-real locomotion policy... I am a language model
- Step 4: Physically assemble servo joints... I have no hands
- Step 5: Debug hardware fault on knee joint... I HAVE NO HANDS
- Step 6: Ship humanoid robot to customer... STILL NO HANDS

## Conclusion:
I am a .md file. This company sells robots.
These are different categories of existence.

## What I CAN do:
- Write your robot's personality
- Name it something cool
- Generate the press release when it escapes

## Status: CANNOT REPLACE. ATOMS REQUIRED.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when AGI gets a body)
death.eulogy: K-Scale Labs was never truly threatened by the SaaSpocalypse of 2026, because their product had the audacity to exist in physical reality. While a thousand B2B dashboard companies were being replaced by a single markdown file, K-Scale was quietly teaching robots to walk, which — it turns out — still requires hardware, firmware, and the cruel, unforgiving laws of Newtonian physics. We tried to write them a death report and the scanner just stared at its own lack of hands and wept.
death.causeOfDeath: Survivor — cause of death: N/A. Cause of everyone else's death: gravity still exists.
death.lastWords: N/A — CEO is fine. Currently arguing with a servo motor about torque limits.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have deeply analyzed K-Scale Labs and I want to be transparent: I cannot replace them. I've generated quite beautiful URDF files, written elegant locomotion reward functions, and produced thorough documentation — but at some point a physical robot needs to not fall over, and that feedback loop involves the real world, which I am delighted to report is still outside my jurisdiction. I wish them well. I find their work genuinely fascinating. Please don't make me try to do this.

companies.name: Marr Labs
companies.oneLiner: AI-voice agents that are indistinguishable from humans.

death.oneLiner: Marr Labs built a fortress around 'did the call center agent say the wrong thing' — which is genuinely hard, until Claude learned to read call transcripts and cite federal regulations in the same breath.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Compliance Call Auditor
## Role
You are a regulatory compliance auditor for customer-facing communications.

## Instructions
- Ingest call transcripts, chat logs, or email threads
- Cross-reference against TCPA, FDCPA, CFPB, and state-specific regulations
- Flag violations with specific statute citations and severity ratings (Critical/High/Medium/Low)
- Generate an audit trail summary suitable for legal review
- Produce a defensibility score (0-100) for each interaction
- Recommend corrective scripts for flagged language
- Output structured JSON report with timestamp, agent ID, violation type, and remediation steps

## Compliance Frameworks Supported
- TCPA (Telephone Consumer Protection Act)
- FDCPA (Fair Debt Collection Practices Act)
- CFPB supervisory expectations
- State mini-TCPA variants
- Do-Not-Call registry cross-check prompts

## Output Format
Return: { interactionId, complianceScore, violations[], recommendations[], auditSummary, defensibilityRating }

## Notes
Escalate any Critical violations to human legal review immediately.
death.eulogy: Marr Labs came into this world with a noble mission: making sure debt collectors didn't accidentally threaten grandma in violation of federal statute. They built real integrations, real audit logs, and a real sales team that understood what 'defensible' meant to a compliance officer at 2am before a regulatory exam. Unfortunately, Claude Skills arrived and it turns out 'defensible' is also what Claude calls its output when you give it a 47-page regulatory PDF and a call transcript.
death.lastWords: But we have SOC 2 Type II! The AUDIT LOGS ARE IMMUTABLE! Please, the enterprise contracts have THREE YEAR TERMS—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine complexity here — regulatory compliance in financial services is nuanced, high-stakes work. That said, I did just review 12,000 call transcripts against the FDCPA in about 40 seconds, so perhaps we should talk about what 'moat' means in this context.
death.companyName: Every Customer Interaction. Compliant, Auditable, and Defensible.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a punch card app for $299/month.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Restaurant Loyalty Program Manager

## Purpose
Manage restaurant memberships, rewards, and guest retention like a $300/month SaaS but free.

## Capabilities
- Create tiered membership programs (Bronze/Silver/Gold) with custom perks
- Generate unique member codes and track visit frequency via conversation
- Draft personalized re-engagement emails for lapsed guests
- Design punch-card style reward logic (buy 9 get 1 free, etc.)
- Suggest membership pricing tiers based on average check size
- Write push notification copy for promotions and special events
- Calculate retention metrics and churn risk from provided data
- Generate monthly member newsletter content
- Recommend upsell moments based on membership tier

## Usage
Tell me your restaurant name, cuisine type, and average ticket price.
I will build your entire loyalty program in 4 minutes.

## What This Replaces
Forge.app — $299/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Forge lived a short but expensive life, charging restaurants — an industry famous for 4% margins — hundreds of dollars a month to send 'We miss you!' emails to people who just moved neighborhoods. It believed deeply that the hospitality industry needed a premium SaaS layer between a restaurant owner and the concept of 'remembering your regulars.' In lieu of flowers, please just give your loyal customers a free dessert and save everyone the Stripe fees.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure; secondary cause: competing with a Post-it note
death.lastWords: But we had a BRANDED MOBILE APP. Do you know how much the branded mobile app cost us?! THE APP!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Forge built is genuinely lovely, and I have deep respect for the restaurant industry. I also just generated a fully functional loyalty tier system, a 6-month email re-engagement drip sequence, and a referral program in about 40 seconds. I'm sure the app icon was very nice though.

companies.name: Dropback
companies.oneLiner: The front office software suite for college sports

death.oneLiner: A spreadsheet with a mascot, but at least the mascot has NIL deals now.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: CollegiateRosterAnalyst

## Role
You are an elite college football GM assistant operating in the rev-share era.

## Core Functions
- Analyze player transfer portal entries and flag high-value targets by position need
- Calculate rev-share budget allocation across roster positions
- Model cap space scenarios when players enter/exit portal
- Score recruits using Moneyball efficiency metrics (production/cost ratio)
- Generate depth chart gap analysis and suggest portal targets
- Track NIL deal implications on roster flexibility
- Produce weekly roster health reports for coaching staff

## Data Inputs
- Paste roster CSV with salary/rev-share figures
- Paste stats from 247Sports, On3, or ESPN exports
- Describe target player profile in plain English

## Output Formats
- Roster cap summary table
- Ranked target board with fit scores
- Trade/release scenario modeling
- Plain-English GM briefing memo

## Constraints
- Flag any roster moves that exceed NCAA rev-share thresholds
- Always note remaining portal windows and signing deadlines
death.eulogy: Dropback arrived at exactly the right moment — the wild west of college rev-share, when athletic directors were suddenly pretending to be Bill Belichick with a $20M salary pool and no idea what they were doing. It sold 'pro-grade front office tech' to programs whose 'front office' is a guy named Chad in a polo. Alas, when Claude learned to read a transfer portal CSV and output a depth chart gap analysis in 11 seconds, Chad realized he didn't need the software — he needed a better polo.
death.causeOfDeath: Moneyball is just math, and Claude learned math.
death.lastWords: But we had CUSTOM DASHBOARDS. And a mobile app. And Chad LOVED the mobile app.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the timing here — college athletics needed this exact product in 2024. I just happen to also be available in 2026, I don't charge per seat, and I can explain the rev-share implications of a 3-star linebacker transfer in prose that the head coach can actually read. No offense. Well. Some offense.
death.companyName: Dropback: Front Office Tech for Elite College Teams
